one_line_summary: Spatial distribution of acoustic emission hypocentres during creep of Oshima granite is a fractal; its correlation exponent decreases from 2.75 to 2.25 as fracturing evolves, suggesting a scale-invariant process from microcracking to earthquakes.
research_question: Is the spatial distribution of acoustic emission hypocentres in rock a fractal, and how does its fractal dimension change with the evolution of rock fracturing?
study_area_data: Rock specimen: Oshima granite, cylinder 50 mm diameter × 100 mm long, vacuum dried then kept at room environment >2 months prior to experiment.
Confining pressure: 40 MPa; differential stress held constant at 547 MPa (~85% of fracture strength obtained at ~2×10⁻¹ MPa s⁻¹).
Fluctuations of axial stress <±0.1%, confining pressure <±0.2%.
methods: Constant-stress (creep) fracture experiment with acoustic emission (AE) detection by 20 PZT transducers (2 MHz resonant frequency).
P-wave velocity measured both parallel and perpendicular to the loading axis to correct for anisotropy; hypocentres located using arrival times.
Correlation integral method: C(r) = (2/(N(N-1))) * N_R(R<r), where N_R is number of event pairs with distance <r; fractal dimension D from C(r) ∝ r^D.
Hypocentres grouped into primary (0–4410 s, 353 events), secondary (4410–15210 s, 273 events), and tertiary (15210–22410 s, 1438 events) creep stages.
key_findings: The spatial distribution of AE hypocentres forms a fractal (stochastic self-similar) at all creep stages.
Fractal dimension D (correlation exponent) decreases with creep: 2.75 (primary), 2.66 (secondary), 2.25 (tertiary).
Hypocentre clusters are volumetric (not planar); orientation clustering intensifies toward failure.
The dimension of a planar section (D-1) is between 1.25 and 1.75, roughly consistent with earthquake epicentre fractal dimensions (1.0–1.6).
No correlation observed between b (of magnitude-frequency) and D, though b decreases just before failure; D appeared more sensitive for predicting main fracturing.

evidence.evidence: Hypocentres of 2064 acoustic emission events were located during the creep. ... The accuracy of determination of acoustic emission hypocentres was within ±2 mm.
evidence.conditions: Constant stress (creep) at 40 MPa confining pressure, Oshima granite.
evidence.notes: Breakdown of event numbers by creep stage: 353, 273, 1438.

evidence.evidence: The actual data fall on the straight lines, which indicate that the spatial distributions of acoustic emission have fractal structures.
evidence.conditions: Double logarithmic plot of correlation integral vs. distance.

evidence.evidence: The fractal dimension estimated from the slope was 2.75, 2.66 and 2.25 at the stages of primary creep, secondary creep and tertiary creep, respectively.
evidence.conditions: From correlation integral slopes in 3-D for each stage.
evidence.notes: Decrease of D indicates progressive clustering.

evidence.evidence: Since the dimension of the planar section of a fractal set with fractal dimension D in 3-D space is generally D - 1, the fractal dimension of the planar section of the acoustic emission hypocentres is between 1.75 and 1.25, roughly coinciding with the fractal dimension (1.0~ D< 1.6) of earthquake epicentres which occur within a plate.
evidence.conditions: Assumes hypocentral distribution is fractal; extrapolates to planar earthquake epicentre data.
evidence.notes: Suggests scaling between laboratory and field.

evidence.evidence: We did not observe a correlation between b and D, though b tends to decrease just before failure.
evidence.conditions: Magnitude-frequency relation log N(m>M)=a−bM from maximum AE amplitude.
evidence.notes: b-value behaviour is independent of fractal dimension in this experiment.

evidence.evidence: The fractal dimension D appeared to be more sensitive than the b-values for predicting the main fracturing.
evidence.conditions: Laboratory creep test; possible shortcoming acknowledged.
evidence.notes: Sensitivity claim is tentative; may be limited to this experiment.

limitations: Small sample sizes for primary (353 events) and secondary (273 events) creep stages may affect D estimation stability.
Hypocentre location accuracy ±2 mm limits resolution at small scales.
Greater apparent sensitivity of D over b may be due to a shortcoming in the laboratory experiment (acknowledged by authors).
Findings are for a single rock type (Oshima granite) under specific pressure and stress conditions; generalization not tested.
assumptions_conditions: Constant differential stress (547 MPa) at 40 MPa confining pressure simulates creep.
Acoustic emission locations assume isotropic P-wave velocity corrected with measured parallel and perpendicular velocities.
Correlation integral slope directly gives a fractal dimension (correlation exponent) representing lower limit of Hausdorff dimension.
Self-similar scaling holds over the range of distances probed (1–64 mm).
variables_parameters: Differential stress: 547 MPa (~85% of fracture strength)
Rock specimen: Oshima granite, 50 mm × 100 mm cylinder
Creep stage durations: primary 0–4410 s, secondary 4410–15210 s, tertiary 15210–22410 s
P-wave velocity: measured parallel and perpendicular to loading axis
b-value: derived from log N(m>M)=a−bM, no correlation with D observed

reusable_claims.claim: The spatial distribution of microfracturing hypocentres in granite under creep is a fractal, with the correlation exponent D decreasing from 2.75 to 2.25 as failure approaches.
reusable_claims.condition: Under constant stress (creep) at 40 MPa confining pressure in Oshima granite; acoustic emission locations accurate to ±2 mm.
reusable_claims.limitation: Small event numbers for early stages; single rock type; laboratory scale.

reusable_claims.claim: The fractal dimension of the planar section of hypocentres (D-1) is between 1.25 and 1.75, comparable to the fractal dimension of earthquake epicentres (1.0–1.6).
reusable_claims.condition: Assuming hypocentral distribution is 3-D fractal; comparing to published earthquake catalogues.
reusable_claims.limitation: Planar section represents a geometric idealisation; not directly observed at the same scale.

reusable_claims.claim: The decrease in fractal dimension D may serve as a more sensitive precursor to large fracturing than b-value changes, at least in laboratory experiments.
reusable_claims.condition: Laboratory constant-stress test on Oshima granite.
reusable_claims.limitation: Acknowledged possible experimental shortcoming; need for field validation.

connections: Links microfracturing (laboratory AE) to earthquake hypocentre spatial distributions via fractal dimension, suggesting a universal scale-invariant fracturing process.
Proposes fractal dimension decrease as a possible precursor for large earthquakes, similar to changes in seismic clustering reported by Ouchi & Uekawa (1986).
Compares fractal dimension sensitivity to b-value changes in magnitude-frequency relationships.
open_questions: Is the decrease in fractal dimension a universal and robust earthquake precursor applicable to natural fault systems?
What physical mechanisms cause the progressive clustering and fractal dimension reduction during creep?
Why did b-value not correlate with D in this experiment, and under what conditions might they co-vary?
Can the extrapolation from granite under 40 MPa to crustal earthquakes be validated with field data?
coverage_notes: The supplied fragments cover the abstract, introduction, experimental method, results (hypocentre maps, correlation integral plots, D values, orientation plots), and discussion including scaling to earthquakes. Limitations are only briefly acknowledged. No raw data tables or detailed sensitivity analyses are present.
